title: SLAAC Address Assignment Monitoring
dataSources: `index=network sourcetype=cisco:ios` for Router Advertisement (RA) syslog messages containing prefix, lifetime, and flag information. Key IOS mnemonics: `%IPV6_ND-6-RA_SEND`, `%IPV6_ND-6-RA_RECEIVE`. On Juniper: `rpd[]: RPD_IPV6_ND_RA_SEND`. RA fields of interest: `prefix` (advertised /64), `preferred_lifetime`, `valid_lifetime`, `M-flag` (Managed address config), `O-flag` (Other stateful config), `A-flag` (Autonomous address config). For passive RA capture: `index=ids sourcetype=zeek:conn` with IPv6 RA detection or Splunk Stream with ICMPv6 Type 134 capture. Volume: one RA per router per interface every 200-600 seconds (RFC 4861 default MaxRtrAdvInterval=600s). A campus with 100 L3 interfaces generates ~600-1,800 RA events/hour.
app: Cisco Networks Add-on (`TA-cisco_ios`, Splunkbase 1352) for syslog, Zeek TA for passive RA capture, or Splunk App for Stream for wire-data ICMPv6 capture.
spl:
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6_ND" OR "Router Advertisement" earliest=-24h
| rex "prefix\s+(?<ra_prefix>[0-9a-fA-F:]+/\d+).*valid\s+(?<valid_lifetime>\d+).*preferred\s+(?<preferred_lifetime>\d+)"
| rex "(?<m_flag>M-flag=[01]).*(?<o_flag>O-flag=[01]).*(?<a_flag>A-flag=[01])"
| eval m_bit=if(match(m_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval o_bit=if(match(o_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval a_bit=if(match(a_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval config_method=case(
    m_bit=1 AND a_bit=0, "DHCPv6 only (stateful)",
    m_bit=0 AND a_bit=1 AND o_bit=0, "SLAAC only",
    m_bit=0 AND a_bit=1 AND o_bit=1, "SLAAC + DHCPv6 for DNS/options",
    m_bit=1 AND a_bit=1, "SLAAC + DHCPv6 (redundant — may confuse clients)",
    1==1, "Unclear")
| stats latest(valid_lifetime) as valid_sec latest(preferred_lifetime) as pref_sec latest(config_method) as method count as ra_count by host, interface, ra_prefix
| eval valid_days=round(valid_sec/86400, 1)
| eval pref_days=round(pref_sec/86400, 1)
| eval lifetime_issue=case(
    valid_sec < 7200, "WARNING: valid lifetime < 2h (too short for renumbering)",
    pref_sec > valid_sec, "ERROR: preferred > valid (RFC violation)",
    valid_sec=4294967295, "INFO: infinite lifetime (permanent prefix)",
    1==1, "OK")
| sort host, interface
description: Monitors IPv6 Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) by tracking Router Advertisements (RAs) from all Layer 3 interfaces — the prefixes being advertised, their valid and preferred lifetimes, and the M/O/A flag combination that determines whether clients use SLAAC, DHCPv6, or both. This is the IPv6 equivalent of monitoring DHCP scope configuration, except SLAAC is decentralised: every router interface independently advertises prefixes, and a misconfigured RA can cause thousands of clients to acquire wrong addresses, lose connectivity, or create split-brain addressing where some clients use SLAAC and others use DHCPv6.
value: A rogue or misconfigured RA is the single most common cause of IPv6 outages in enterprise networks. Unlike DHCP where a central server provides control, any device sending RAs becomes a de facto router for that segment. This UC catches: prefix mismatches (wrong /64 advertised), lifetime problems (too-short preferred lifetime causes address thrashing, preferred > valid violates RFC 4861), flag inconsistencies (M=1 and A=1 simultaneously confuses Windows and macOS clients differently), and RA frequency anomalies (too many RAs waste wireless client battery per RFC 7772). Without monitoring, these misconfigurations persist until users report connectivity issues, which may be intermittent and difficult to reproduce.
implementation: Enable IPv6 ND logging on routers (`debug ipv6 nd` for troubleshooting, or rely on standard syslog for steady-state). The search parses RA syslog messages to extract prefix, lifetimes, and flags, then classifies each RA by its address configuration method and checks for known problems. Schedule hourly for ongoing monitoring; run on-demand during IPv6 troubleshooting.
visualization: (1) Table: router, interface, prefix, valid/preferred lifetimes, config method (SLAAC/DHCPv6/both), lifetime issues — sorted by issues first. (2) Single-value tiles: interfaces with lifetime issues, interfaces with flag inconsistencies. (3) Timechart: RA rate per router/interface over 24h — spikes indicate rogue RAs or RA storms. (4) Status grid: green (OK), yellow (INFO), red (WARNING/ERROR) per interface.

references.title: RFC 4861 — Neighbor Discovery for IP version 6 (IPv6) — Router Advertisement specification
RFC 9099 — Operational Security Considerations for IPv6 Networks (§2.1 — Address assignment)
RFC 7772 — Reducing Energy Consumption of Router Advertisements (BCP 202 — RA rate limiting for wireless)
RFC 8981 — Temporary Address Extensions for Stateless Address Autoconfiguration in IPv6

knownFalsePositives:
**Multiple RAs from the same router on VRRP/HSRP failover.** During first-hop redundancy failover, both the old and new active router may send RAs briefly. This creates a spike in RA count but is transient and normal. Distinguish by checking if the RA source link-local address changes (new router) vs stays the same (same router re-advertising).

**Different RA frequencies across vendors.** Cisco defaults to MaxRtrAdvInterval=200s, Juniper to 600s, Arista to 200s. Different RA frequencies from different routers on the same VLAN are expected in multi-vendor environments and don't indicate a problem unless the advertised prefixes or flags differ.

**Infinite lifetimes (4294967295).** Some deployments intentionally set infinite valid lifetime for static infrastructure prefixes. This isn't wrong, but it means the prefix will persist even if RAs stop — which is correct for infrastructure but could be problematic for client subnets where you need the ability to renumber.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• IPv6 is configured on router interfaces with SLAAC-advertised prefixes. Verify: `show ipv6 interface <intf>` should show "Hosts use stateless autoconfig for addresses" and list the prefix being advertised.
• Syslog from routers must include ND-related messages. On Cisco IOS-XE: syslog facility `IPV6_ND` messages at severity 6 (informational). Ensure `logging trap informational` or more verbose is configured.
• For enhanced RA capture (optional): deploy Zeek sensor or Splunk Stream on a SPAN/TAP port to capture ICMPv6 Type 134 (Router Advertisement) packets at wire level. This catches RAs from ALL sources, including rogue devices, not just from managed routers.
• Understanding of your intended SLAAC configuration per VLAN:
  - Which prefix should each interface advertise?
  - What are the intended M/O/A flag settings?
  - What valid/preferred lifetimes are configured?
  Document these in a lookup (`slaac_expected_config.csv`: `host, interface, expected_prefix, expected_m_flag, expected_o_flag, expected_a_flag`) for automated compliance checking.

Step 1 — Configure data collection

Syslog from routers is typically already collected (prerequisite for most UC-5.1.x UCs). Verify RA-related syslog messages are arriving:
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6_ND" earliest=-24h | stats count by host
```

If zero events but IPv6 is configured, check:
1. Syslog level: `logging trap informational` on the router (IPV6_ND messages are severity 6).
2. RA frequency: with default MaxRtrAdvInterval=200s and MinRtrAdvInterval=0.33×200=66s, a router sends approximately 6-15 RAs per hour per interface. If you're checking a short window (< 30 minutes), you may need to wait.

For enhanced monitoring, enable ND event logging on Cisco:
```
ipv6 nd log
```
This generates a syslog message for every RA sent, which provides consistent logging regardless of syslog level.

For passive RA capture with Zeek:
```
# site/local.zeek
@load protocols/conn/known-services
event icmp_sent(c: connection, icmp: icmp_conn) {
    if (c$id$resp_h == ff02::1 && icmp$itype == 134) {
        # Router Advertisement detected
    }
}
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

**Primary search — SLAAC configuration audit:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6_ND" OR "Router Advertisement" earliest=-24h
| rex "prefix\s+(?<ra_prefix>[0-9a-fA-F:]+/\d+).*valid\s+(?<valid_lifetime>\d+).*preferred\s+(?<preferred_lifetime>\d+)"
| rex "(?<m_flag>M-flag=[01]).*(?<o_flag>O-flag=[01]).*(?<a_flag>A-flag=[01])"
| where isnotnull(ra_prefix)
| eval m_bit=if(match(m_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval o_bit=if(match(o_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval a_bit=if(match(a_flag, "=1"), 1, 0)
| eval config_method=case(
    m_bit=1 AND a_bit=0, "DHCPv6 only (stateful)",
    m_bit=0 AND a_bit=1 AND o_bit=0, "SLAAC only",
    m_bit=0 AND a_bit=1 AND o_bit=1, "SLAAC + DHCPv6 for DNS/options",
    m_bit=1 AND a_bit=1, "SLAAC + DHCPv6 (dual — may confuse clients)",
    m_bit=0 AND a_bit=0, "No address assignment (RA for routing only)",
    1==1, "Unknown")
| stats latest(valid_lifetime) as valid_sec latest(preferred_lifetime) as pref_sec latest(config_method) as method count as ra_count by host, interface, ra_prefix
| eval valid_days=round(valid_sec/86400, 1)
| eval pref_days=round(pref_sec/86400, 1)
| eval issues=mvappend(
    if(valid_sec < 7200, "valid lifetime < 2h", null()),
    if(pref_sec > valid_sec, "preferred > valid (RFC violation)", null()),
    if(m_bit=1 AND a_bit=1, "M+A both set (confusing)", null()),
    if(pref_sec < 3600, "preferred lifetime < 1h (address thrashing risk)", null()))
| eval issue_count=mvcount(issues)
| eval status=if(issue_count > 0, "ISSUE", "OK")
| sort -issue_count, host, interface
```

**Understanding this SPL:**
- The regex extracts prefix, valid lifetime, and preferred lifetime from RA syslog messages. The format varies by platform — Cisco IOS-XE uses `prefix <prefix> valid <seconds> preferred <seconds>`.
- M/O/A flags determine the address configuration method. The most common enterprise deployments use A=1, O=1, M=0 (SLAAC for addresses + DHCPv6 for DNS/NTP options). M=1 with A=1 is technically valid but causes different behaviour across OS vendors.
- `mvappend()` creates a multi-value list of all issues found for each RA configuration, allowing a single row to show multiple problems.

**Alert — RA configuration anomaly:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6_ND" earliest=-1h
| rex "prefix\s+(?<ra_prefix>[0-9a-fA-F:]+/\d+).*valid\s+(?<valid_lifetime>\d+).*preferred\s+(?<preferred_lifetime>\d+)"
| where isnotnull(ra_prefix) AND (preferred_lifetime > valid_lifetime OR valid_lifetime < 3600)
| stats count by host, interface, ra_prefix, valid_lifetime, preferred_lifetime
```
Trigger: any result. `preferred > valid` is an RFC 4861 violation. `valid < 1h` causes addresses to expire quickly, risking connectivity loss.

**RA rate anomaly detection (rogue RA indicator):**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%IPV6_ND" earliest=-1h
| timechart span=5m count by host
| where count > 50
```
Trigger: > 50 RA-related events in 5 minutes from any router. Normal rate is ~1-2 RAs per interface per 200-600 seconds. High rates indicate RA flooding (UC-5.20.21) or debug/logging misconfiguration.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **CLI verification:** On a Cisco router: `show ipv6 nd interface <intf>`. Compare prefix, lifetimes, and flags against the Splunk output. They should match.

(b) **Client-side verification:** On a Linux client: `rdisc6 <interface>` or `ip -6 addr show`. The client's SLAAC-assigned address prefix should match the RA prefix shown in Splunk. If the client has an unexpected prefix, it's receiving RAs from a different source (rogue RA — see UC-5.20.21).

(c) **Flag consistency across VLANs:** Run the search grouped by `ra_prefix` (not host) and check that all routers on the same VLAN advertise the same M/O/A flags and the same lifetimes. Inconsistency causes some clients to use SLAAC and others to use DHCPv6, depending on which RA they receive first.

(d) **Lifetime reasonableness:** Valid lifetime of 2592000 (30 days) and preferred lifetime of 604800 (7 days) are the RFC 4861 defaults. Significantly shorter lifetimes should have a documented reason (e.g., renumbering-ready configuration per RFC 9096).

Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard** ("IPv6 — SLAAC Configuration Health"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: interfaces with issues (red if > 0), total RA-active interfaces, interfaces with flag inconsistency across routers.
• Row 2 — Table: full audit results sorted by issues first. Drilldown to per-router detail.
• Row 3 — Timechart: RA event rate per router over 24h.

**Scheduling:** Hourly audit. Real-time alert for `preferred > valid` violation.

**Runbook** (owner: Network Engineering):
1. `preferred > valid`: immediate fix. This is an RFC violation that causes unpredictable client behaviour. Set `preferred ≤ valid` on the router interface: `ipv6 nd prefix <prefix> <valid> <preferred>`.
2. Flag inconsistency across routers on same VLAN: standardise. Decide on SLAAC-only vs SLAAC+DHCPv6 and configure all routers on that VLAN identically.
3. Short lifetimes (< 2h valid): if intentional (renumbering readiness), document. If not, increase to standard defaults (30d valid, 7d preferred).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No RA syslog messages** — (1) IPv6 may not be configured on any router interface: `show ipv6 interface brief` should show interfaces with global addresses. (2) Syslog level too restrictive: `show logging | include trap` must be `informational` or more verbose. (3) RA suppression: `ipv6 nd ra suppress all` disables RAs on an interface (used on host-only segments). This is intentional but means no SLAAC monitoring is possible on that interface.

• **Regex doesn't match** — RA syslog format varies by platform and IOS version. Run `index=network sourcetype=cisco:ios "%IPV6_ND" | head 5` and examine the raw format. Adjust the regex to match your platform's output format.

• **Multiple prefixes per interface** — A single interface can advertise multiple /64 prefixes (e.g., for renumbering or multi-homing). Each prefix generates a separate row in the results. This is architecturally valid but each prefix should be in your prefix plan (UC-5.20.8).

• **RA rate is zero but clients have SLAAC addresses** — The router sent RAs in the past (clients cached the prefix) but has since stopped. Check if the router was reloaded, the interface was shut/no-shut, or RA suppression was applied. Clients will keep their addresses until the valid lifetime expires.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the announcements that routers make to tell computers on the network what their address should be — like a post office announcing the area's ZIP code. If a router announces the wrong code, or announces it too often, or stops announcing it, computers end up with wrong addresses and can't communicate properly.
